result:
:::writing
The first time the city forgot my name, I didn’t notice.

It happened on a Tuesday, the kind that carries no weight in memory, the kind that arrives politely and leaves without saying goodbye. I woke up to the hum of the refrigerator and the faint rattle of pipes in the walls, a mechanical chorus that sounded almost like breathing. I went through the motions—coffee, shower, keys in the same chipped bowl by the door—and stepped outside into a morning that felt exactly like every other.

It wasn’t until the barista looked at me—really looked, with that polite squint of recognition failing to ignite—and asked, “Name for the order?” that something shifted.

I told her.

She nodded, wrote something on the cup, and when she called it out a few minutes later, it wasn’t mine.

At least, I didn’t think it was. The sound of it felt wrong in the air, like a note slightly off-key. But no one else moved to claim the drink, and the barista looked directly at me, eyebrows raised in gentle expectation.

“Isn’t this yours?” she asked.

I hesitated. It was such a small thing. Names slip all the time. People mishear, miswrite, misremember. It’s part of the soft chaos of being human. So I took the cup, nodded, and said thank you.

The name written there wasn’t one I recognized.

I told myself it didn’t matter.

But then the bus driver didn’t look at me when I got on, not even in that brief transactional way that acknowledges a shared moment. My coworker, Elise, greeted me with a cheerful, “Hey you,” instead of my name, which she usually stretched into two syllables as a kind of inside joke.

“Morning,” I said.

“Morning,” she echoed, already turning back to her screen.

I stood there for a second longer than necessary, waiting for the rest of it—for her to remember, to correct herself, to say my name like she always did. But it didn’t come.

The day unfolded in small omissions. Emails addressed without names. A meeting where everyone was introduced except me, as if I were already known or not worth knowing. Even my reflection in the bathroom mirror seemed slightly off, like a photograph taken a fraction of a second too late.

By the time I got home, I was carrying a quiet, growing unease.

I checked my wallet.

My ID was there, tucked behind a stack of receipts and an old movie ticket. I slid it out and looked at it carefully. The photo was mine, or close enough. The address was right. The date of birth matched.

But where my name should have been, there was only a blank line.

Not scratched out. Not smudged. Just… empty.

I laughed then, a short, sharp sound that didn’t quite convince even me. “Okay,” I said to the empty apartment. “Very funny.”

I checked my phone. Contacts, messages, social media—everywhere my name should appear, it didn’t. In group chats, my messages floated in anonymous bubbles. My email signature had collapsed into nothing.

It was as if someone had carefully, surgically removed every instance of my name from the world.

I tried to say it out loud.

My mouth opened. My tongue moved. But no sound came.

Not silence exactly—more like a placeholder where a sound should be, a gap shaped like a word that refused to exist.

I sat down on the edge of the bed, suddenly aware of how quiet the apartment was. Not the comfortable quiet of solitude, but a hollow kind, like the space left behind after something essential has been taken.

“Okay,” I said again, softer this time. “Okay.”

There are practical problems when you don’t have a name.

The next morning, I couldn’t log into my work account. The system required a username, and whatever mine had been, it was gone. IT couldn’t help me without “verifying identity,” which apparently hinged on the very thing I no longer had.

“Can you just tell me your name?” the technician asked over the phone.

“I—” I tried again, forcing the word up from wherever it had hidden. Nothing. Just that same empty shape.

“Hello?” he said. “Are you still there?”

“Yes,” I said. “I just… I can’t.”

There was a pause. “You can’t tell me your name?”

“No.”

Another pause, longer this time. I could hear him shifting in his chair, the faint creak of something under his weight.

“Okay,” he said slowly. “Well, without that, I’m not sure what I can do.”

“Right,” I said. “Of course.”

After that, things unraveled quickly.

Without a name, I was difficult to account for. My bank flagged my account for “inconsistencies.” My landlord sent a notice addressed to “Current Occupant.” Even my doctor’s office couldn’t find my records, though they assured me that didn’t mean they didn’t exist—just that they couldn’t locate them.

It was as if I had slipped sideways out of the systems that define a life.

People still saw me. They interacted with me. But there was a subtle shift, a lack of anchoring. Conversations drifted. Eye contact didn’t quite hold. Without a name, I became harder to grasp, even for myself.

I started to wonder what a name really does.

It’s more than a label. It’s a point of return, a way for the world to call you back into itself. Without it, every interaction feels provisional, like you’re borrowing a place you haven’t earned.

I tried to choose a new one.

I stood in front of the mirror and said different names, testing them like clothes that didn’t quite fit. Some felt too heavy, others too thin. None of them stuck. They slid off me, leaving no trace.

Days passed. Or maybe weeks. Time became slippery without the usual markers.

One evening, I went back to the coffee shop.

The same barista was there. She smiled when I walked in, a small flicker of recognition that felt like a lifeline.

“Hey,” she said. “Good to see you again.”

“Good to see you too,” I replied.

“Name for the order?”

I hesitated.

There it was again, the gap. The place where something should be.

But this time, instead of trying to reach for what was missing, I did something else.

I said, “I don’t have one.”

She blinked. Not confused, exactly. Just surprised.

“Okay,” she said slowly. “Do you want to… pick one?”

I considered it.

All those names I had tried, all of them falling away. The idea of choosing one now felt different—not like trying to recover something lost, but like deciding something new.

“Not yet,” I said.

She nodded, as if that made perfect sense. “Alright. I’ll just call you… this one,” she added, pointing gently at me.

It was a strange compromise, but it worked.

When she called out the drink, she didn’t use a name. She just looked at me, lifted the cup slightly, and smiled.

And I knew, without doubt, that it was mine.

I took it, feeling something settle into place—not the old certainty, but something quieter, more flexible. A recognition that didn’t rely on a word.

The city still didn’t know my name.

But it knew me in other ways—the rhythm of my footsteps, the pattern of my days, the way I paused at crosswalks even when the street was empty.

And maybe that was enough, at least for now.

Maybe a name isn’t the only way to exist.

Maybe it’s just one way of being held.

And maybe, someday, I’ll choose one again.

Or maybe I won’t.

Either way, I’m still here.
:::writing

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
